turns.content: i need you to help me understand the brachial plexus because ive been staring at this diagram for 2 hours and i want to cry

The brachial plexus is one of those topics that makes everyone want to cry at some point, so you're in good company. Where are you getting stuck -- is it the roots and trunks, the divisions and cords, or the terminal branches?

all of it bruv. like i can remember its C5 to T1 and then it goes roots trunks divisions cords branches but then when i try to remember WHICH nerves come off WHERE my brain just goes blank. like the lateral cord gives off the musculocutaneous and part of the median right? or is that the medial cord i literally cant keep them straight

You're actually closer than you think. Let me break it down.

The three cords are named by their position relative to the axillary artery -- lateral, medial, and posterior. Here's what comes off each:

**Lateral cord** (C5, C6, C7): Lateral pectoral nerve, musculocutaneous nerve, and the lateral contribution to the median nerve.

**Medial cord** (C8, T1): Medial pectoral nerve, medial cutaneous nerves of the arm and forearm, ulnar nerve, and the medial contribution to the median nerve.

**Posterior cord** (C5-T1): Upper and lower subscapular nerves, thoracodorsal nerve, axillary nerve, and radial nerve.

The median nerve is the one that trips everyone up because it gets contributions from both the lateral AND medial cords -- they join together like a fork. That's why it's easy to confuse which cord it belongs to.

okay so the median nerve is like a collab between the lateral and medial cords. thats actually helpful when you put it like that. but then how do i remember which cord gives off which terminal branches because theres so many

Mnemonics help a lot here. For the terminal branches, try thinking of it this way:

**Lateral cord** -- think "LML": Lateral pectoral, Musculocutaneous, Lateral contribution to median.

**Medial cord** -- think "M-MUMM": Medial pectoral, Medial cutaneous arm, Medial cutaneous forearm, Ulnar, Medial contribution to median.

**Posterior cord** -- think "ULTRA": Upper subscapular, Lower subscapular, Thoracodorsal, Radial, Axillary.

The posterior cord is the easiest because its two big terminal branches are the radial and axillary nerves -- the two nerves that deal with the back of the arm and the shoulder.

ULTRA is actually hard. wait let me write that down. upper subscapular lower subscapular thoracodorsal radial axillary. yeah okay i can work with that. what about the actual roots to trunks bit though because superior trunk is C5 C6 yeah? and then what

Yeah exactly:

- **Superior trunk**: C5 + C6
- **Middle trunk**: C7 (just continues on its own)
- **Inferior trunk**: C8 + T1

The middle trunk is easy because it's just C7 doing its own thing. Then each trunk splits into an anterior and posterior division. All three posterior divisions come together to form the posterior cord. The anterior divisions of the superior and middle trunks form the lateral cord, and the anterior division of the inferior trunk forms the medial cord.

So the naming makes sense -- the posterior cord gets all the posterior divisions, the lateral cord is from the upper two trunks' anterior divisions, and the medial cord is from the bottom trunk's anterior division.

okay actually when you explain it like that with the logic behind the naming it makes way more sense than just trying to memorise a diagram. because the names literally tell you where they are. lateral is lateral medial is medial posterior gets all the posterior divisions. why doesnt the textbook just say that

Textbooks love making simple logic look complicated with dense diagrams and formal language. The anatomy itself is logical -- everything is named for where it is or what it does. Once you see the pattern, you stop memorising and start understanding. The brachial plexus is really just a sorting system that takes five nerve roots and reorganises them into specific nerves for the upper limb.

a sorting system. thats mad i never thought of it like that. its like the nerve roots get mixed and matched so the right fibres end up in the right nerve going to the right muscle. yeah okay my brain is actually clicking now. let me test myself. so the musculocutaneous nerve comes off the lateral cord and it does biceps and brachialis yeah? the flexors of the elbow

Spot on. Musculocutaneous nerve from the lateral cord -- it innervates biceps brachii, brachialis, and coracobrachialis (the three muscles of the anterior arm compartment), and then it continues as the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm for sensation. You've got it.
and then the radial nerve is posterior cord and it does all the extensors yeah? triceps and all the wrist and finger extensors

Exactly right. Radial nerve from the posterior cord is the nerve of the extensors. Triceps in the arm, then all the extensors in the posterior forearm -- wrist extension, finger extension, supination via supinator. It's the biggest branch of the posterior cord and the biggest nerve in the brachial plexus overall. If you remember 'posterior cord = posterior muscles = extensors = radial nerve,' that logic chain holds.

bruv i think ive actually learned the brachial plexus in 20 minutes when ive been failing at it for weeks. the sorting system thing and the ULTRA mnemonic are doing the heavy lifting tbh. i need to go draw this out like 5 times now while its fresh. thank you man seriously this was doing my absolute head in

Drawing it out is exactly the right move -- do it from memory each time and check yourself after. By the fifth time it'll be locked in. The brachial plexus looks scary on a diagram but once you see the logic it's one of the more satisfying things to learn. Good luck with the revision, you've clearly got it now.
